Sitemap, drafted · 1,601 → ~650 pages

An opinionated IA for holyfamily.edu. 

Your current site has 1,601 URLs across 180+ distinct top-level prefixes. Our first-pass recommendation consolidates to eight sections and roughly 650 active pages, a 60 percent reduction. Every URL is justified by a primary audience. We expect half of this to change during Discovery. The other half is load-bearing.

/ holyfamily.edu Target ~650 pages · from 1,601 

/programs/ academic programs hub ~130 pgs 

/programs/undergraduate/ 30+ majors ~45 pgs 

/programs/graduate/ 20+ grad programs ~40 pgs 

/programs/adult-continuing/ ACE + credit for prior learning ~25 pgs 

/programs/online/ online degree paths ~20 pgs 

/admissions/ enrollment hub · one smart RFI form ~45 pgs 

/student-life/ residential, clubs, ministry ~55 pgs 

/about/ mission, leadership, history ~40 pgs 

/news/ 2-year rolling window · 500+ archived ~120 pgs 

/athletics/ 19 varsity programs ~65 pgs 

/alumni/ giving, events, stories ~45 pgs 

/directory/ faculty & staff · single source ~150 pgs 

The 60 percent cut is a cost lever, not a promise to delete content. 500+ news articles older than two years move to an archive subdomain with 301s preserved. 23 duplicate RFI forms collapse to one smart form routed to Slate by program. Duplicate program pages consolidate into canonical parents with sub-tracks. Card sort and tree test validate the remaining IA with real users before Phase 2 closes.

Executive Summary · for the President's Cabinet 
08 / 30 

The thesis of this response

More applications. Specifically adult and graduate. 

The real Holy Family lives on the ground. Ninety-three percent of graduates placed into full-time work within six months. Forty percent of the student body studying Nursing and Health Sciences. The Sisters of the Holy Family of Nazareth still shaping a 1954 founding into a vibrant Catholic learning community. That university does not live on holyfamily.edu today, and the gap between the two is the reason prospective adult and graduate applicants leave without applying. Closing the gap is the entire work of this proposal. Conservative math on page sixteen shows a $2.5M year-one return on a $235K investment.

Commitment 01 · enrollment

Adult & graduate applications, up 40%. 

Your RFP names Adult and Graduate enrollment as the top growth priority. We draft Variant B, an entire homepage built for a 34-year-old Nursing applicant on a phone at 9 PM, and ship it in base scope. Four specific conversion moves on page 09.

See pages 09–11, 16, 17

Commitment 02 · collaboration

Your team is a co-builder, not a client.

Rick Mitchell's team gets shared GitHub write access at kickoff, not at handoff. Code review in both directions. Weekly working sessions, not status meetings. Your 2025 prep work ingested week one with zero rework.

See page 20

Commitment 03 · AI architecture

llms.txt + schema, drafted for you. 

Half of prospective students already research with ChatGPT and Perplexity. We draft your llms.txt and schema.org pack inside this proposal so when a parent asks "is Holy Family worth it?", the first-surface answer is your 93% employment rate, not a marketing blurb.

See page 15

The RFP's loudest signal · Adult & Graduate enrollment 
09 / 30 

RFP Section 4 · Goal 2 · the priority you named

Four moves that turn browsers into applicants. 

Section 4 of your RFP names Adult and Graduate enrollment as the top growth priority. These applicants browse from phones at night between shifts, compare five schools in a single sitting, and need cost and schedule visible above the fold. The four tactics below are the specific moves we will ship to convert them, not observations about who they are.

Move 01 · Decision info above the fold

Tuition, schedule, time-to-degree on the hero. 

No "request info to see pricing." No gated PDFs. Every adult + graduate program page shows total cost, per-credit cost, average time to complete part-time, and three career outcomes with salary ranges before any scroll. Expected lift: +25–35% application-start rate on program pages. 

Move 02 · One smart RFI form

23 duplicates collapse to one. 

Your current site has 23 separate request-information forms scattered across program pages. We collapse them into one Gravity Form with program routing, conditional questions, and real-time Slate webhook delivery. Expected lift: +15–20% inquiry volume, 100% reduction in form abandonment from routing confusion. 

Move 03 · Variant B adult hero

A homepage drafted for them. 

Page 11 shows the drafted adult/graduate homepage hero. "Finish what you started. On your schedule." Credit for prior learning. Time-to-degree. Cost up front. Switched via UTM + returning-visitor behavior. No other agency will ship this in base scope. Expected lift: +40–60% on adult/grad landing from paid channels. 

Move 04 · Slate funnel measurement

From visit to deposit, attributed. 

GA4 four-stage funnel (awareness → consideration → decision → conversion), Slate Ping on every page, cross-domain tracking through the application portal, and a Looker Studio dashboard Katherine Primus reads herself. See page 17. Expected outcome: first clear ROI attribution on marketing spend. 

RFP Section 4 · Project Goals · "specific focus on improving enrollment in Adult and Graduate programs"

Section G.2 · Cultural fit & values 
13 / 30 

Mission is not boilerplate

Founded in 1954 by the Sisters of the Holy Family of Nazareth. 

Holy Family University is a vibrant Catholic learning community that educates the whole person for a life of integrity, service, and personal excellence. We quote your mission statement here because almost every agency will quote it back as a setup for a generic "inclusive design" pitch. We read it differently. It is an instruction manual for how this site should be built.

Whole person

Academic rigor and campus ministry, co-equal.

Most university sites treat mission as a sidebar. We treat it as a first-class content type. Mission stories sit in the same editorial grid as program stats. Campus ministry and service-learning are findable from primary nav.

First lay president

A modern voice, rooted in tradition. 

Dr. Anne Prisco is the first lay president in Holy Family's history, leading a strategic plan that expires the same month this redesign kicks off. The website is the visible deliverable that signals the next era. We write copy that honors the Sisters' founding charism while speaking to a 17-year-old deciding where to apply.

40% Nursing

The single biggest enrollment driver. 

Forty percent of students study Nursing or Health Sciences. The current site buries it in a program list. The redesign gives Nursing a flagship page with outcomes, NCLEX pass rates, clinical partnerships, and the top three applicant questions answered above the fold.

93% employed

Hidden in plain sight. 

Ninety-three percent of graduates employed within six months. Decisive in a cost-conscious environment. Currently almost invisible. The redesign surfaces it in the hero, on program pages, in FAQ schema, and inside the llms.txt canonical facts block.

RFP Section G.2 · Cultural Fit and Values · mission alignment as strategy, not stamp

RFP Section 6 · Enrollment funnel measurement 
17 / 30 

GA4 · GTM · Slate · Looker Studio

A four-stage funnel, measured end to end. 

Awareness → consideration → decision → conversion. Every stage tracked, every source attributed, every lift measurable. We preserve your existing GA4 and GTM container ( GTM-5FPBQK3 ) and extend it with a framework Katherine Primus's team reads directly in Looker Studio.

Stage 01 · Awareness

They find you. 

Program page views, video plays, blog engagement, time on site. Sources attributed: organic, AI referral, paid, social, direct.

Target lift: +25% sessions

Stage 02 · Consideration

They compare. 

RFI form starts, campus visit scheduling, financial aid calculator, program comparisons, return visits.

Target lift: +30% RFI submits

Stage 03 · Decision

They commit. 

Apply Now clicks, application portal visits, net-price calculator, scholarship views, cross-domain Slate handoff.

Target lift: +15% app starts

Stage 04 · Conversion

They apply. 

Application submission, deposit payment, enrollment confirmation, attribution back to original source via UTM + Slate Ping.

Target lift: +8% enrollments

Delivered at launch. GA4 preserved and extended with 28 custom events. GTM container retained. Data layer documented. Cross-domain tracking to the Slate application portal. A Looker Studio dashboard with a funnel visualization, source attribution, and program-level drilldown. Plus a 60-minute training session so Katherine Primus's team reads the funnel without calling us. Target lifts above are industry benchmarks for a well-executed higher-ed redesign within twelve months post-launch.

Section B.2–B.3 · Discovery & IA methodology 
18 / 30 

How we listen before we build

Discovery is six weeks of structured listening. 

Ingest the 2025 prep work (week 1). Your content audit, governance guidelines, IA analysis, analytics baselines, and stakeholder surveys become our starting position. Zero rework. Phase 1 closes two weeks earlier than a standard Discovery cycle.

Institutional listening (weeks 1–2). Thirty-minute one-on-ones with Dr. Prisco, Katherine Primus, Rick Mitchell, Mark Green, Edward Wright, and Eric Nelson. Each session recorded, synthesized within 48 hours, validated within 72.

Content audit & AI-assisted classification (weeks 2–3). All 1,601 URLs classified via LLM batch into keep / rewrite / consolidate / archive / delete. Human review on every decision. Auditable worksheet delivered.

Task-based user research (weeks 3–4). 8–10 participants across three segments (prospective undergraduate, prospective adult/graduate, current student). First-click testing on four key tasks: find a program, start an application, look up a professor, find an event.

Information architecture validation (weeks 4–5). Card sort with 15–20 participants. Tree test on the proposed navigation. Baseline comparison against the current IA. The sitemap on page 06 is the first-pass recommendation; Discovery validates it with real users.

Content model & sign-off (weeks 5–6). WordPress custom post types, ACF field groups, taxonomies. Drupal-to-WordPress field mapping. Gate G1 closes the phase with Rick Mitchell and Katherine Primus signing off.

Section B.6 · Content strategy & migration

The real scope is smaller · the real cost is lower

1,601 URLs to approximately 650. 

Migration at the surface is a labor cost. Migration under the surface is content strategy. A sixty percent reduction is not a promise to delete content, it is a commitment to identify what earns its place and protect the URL equity of what does not. AI drafts the classifications. Editors and your team make the final calls. Tests verify nothing breaks.

Action URLs Outcome 

News articles ≥ 2 years old ~500 Archived to /news-archive/ with 301s preserved 

Duplicate request-information forms ~22 One smart form, Slate-routed by program 

Duplicate program landing pages ~80 Canonical parent + sub-track model 

Test, seasonal, expired pages ~40 Deleted with 301s to nearest canonical 

Redundant policy pages ~30 Consolidated under a /policies/ hub 

Top-level URL prefixes 180 → 8 Eight canonical sections, every URL justified 

Net result at launch ~650 60% reduction, every URL given a validated 301 

Tooling: FG Drupal to WordPress Premium for bulk transfer. WP All Import for ACF refinement. Custom scripts for nested Drupal Paragraphs. Semantic 301 mapping via sentence-transformer embeddings (all-MiniLM-L6-v2 + FAISS) auto-accepts matches above 0.98 similarity, manual review below. Post-migration QA compares source to destination on every page.

Collaborative model · 15 percent of the evaluation

Partners, not obstacles

Rick's team builds with us, not receives from us.

Four phrases in your RFP point in the same direction. "Partners, not obstacles." "Working sessions, not just presentations." "Knowledge transfer throughout the project, not just at handoff." "Welcomes input and code review from our developers." Each is specific enough to signal a settled preference. This response addresses each phrase with a concrete ritual rather than an adjective.

Ritual 01 · kickoff day one

Shared GitHub write access. 

Not read access. Not at handoff. Rick's team commits alongside ours from week one. Feature branches, pull requests, issue tracker, project board open to both sides immediately.

Ritual 02 · every pull request

Code review both directions. 

Every PR requires one named reviewer from each side. We review your team's commits. Your team reviews ours. Nothing merges without dual sign-off. Institutional context captured in comments, not tribal memory.

Ritual 03 · every Tuesday

45-minute working pair, not status.

A live session on the hardest decision of the week. Whiteboard the tradeoff, commit the answer to the repo. Rotating attendees so institutional knowledge spreads across the team, not just through Rick.

Ritual 04 · week one

Your 2025 prep, ingested. 

Your 2025 content audit, governance guidelines, IA analysis, stakeholder surveys, and analytics baselines are ingested during the first week with zero rework. Phase 1 closes two weeks earlier than a standard Discovery cycle.

What this means for the Cabinet. When our ninety days of post-launch support end, your team owns this codebase as deeply as any internal team owns a site they built themselves. The next template, the next form, the next section ships without an external support ticket. That is what success looks like on this engagement.

RFP Section A.4 and B.8 · Collaborative Development Experience & Process

Section C.1 · CMS recommendation 
21 / 30 

Traditional WordPress, not headless · honest tradeoff math

WordPress, not headless. 

Your RFP prefers WordPress and explicitly invites a headless vs. traditional rationale. For Rick Mitchell's two-person team running this site for five years, traditional wins on every axis that matters. The largest talent pool, the most mature plugin ecosystem, the lowest total cost of ownership. We are not upselling an architecture your team cannot sustainably operate.

Why WordPress

Editorial usability for a small team. 

Rick's team publishes content, not support tickets. Gutenberg real-time preview, in-context editing, media library. The benchmark for content author experience, especially after WordPress 6.x.

Why WordPress

Largest developer ecosystem. 

When Holy Family needs a developer in three years, WordPress talent is abundant and competitively priced. Drupal talent is smaller and more expensive. Across five years that difference compounds into real dollars.

Why not headless

Editorial workflow degrades. 

Real-time preview and in-context editing require expensive re-engineering. Yoast, Gravity Forms, GTM integration all assume a traditional front-end. Two codebases to maintain. Permanent React/Next.js skills dependency.

Future proof

REST API ready. 

ACF Pro exposes every post type via REST from day one. If HFU ever needs a mobile app, digital signage, or a decoupled front end in the future, the content layer is already headless-ready. You pay for that flexibility only when you need it.

Integrated throughout, not retrofitted

Your RFP insists accessibility be "integrated throughout the design and development process, not retrofitted." That language exists because a prior vendor did the opposite. Automated tools catch 30 to 57 percent of real WCAG failures. The other 70 percent is manual, and it is where we spend real hours.

Design phase. Color contrast validated to 4.5:1 before mockups ship. 44×44 px touch targets in wireframes. Focus states designed as a first-class visual element for every interactive component.

The four mistakes the current site makes

Every scene above traces back to one of four decisions we would undo on day one.

01

It optimizes for the wrong persona. 

The current holyfamily.edu was built for the 18-year-old prospective who arrives in daylight, with a parent, clicking "Apply" as an abstract future verb. Maria is 27, alone, tired, and comparing four schools inside one browser session. Every pixel she lands on assumes she's on a campus tour.

The fix 
A dedicated Adult Learner track in the IA — nights, mobile, price-first, program-first.

02

It hides the price. 

The BSN program page never shows tuition. Not per credit, not per term, not total. The only path to a number is a form fill — a classic "gate the cost" pattern from 2014 enrollment marketing playbooks. Adult learners shopping four schools in one night will not fill out four forms. They will drop you from the list.

The fix 
Price and time-to-degree above the fold on every program page. No exceptions.

03

The RFI form breaks on a phone. 

The current Request Info form is a 12-field, three-column layout that was written for a desktop monitor and never reflowed for mobile. At 390px — the iPhone 14 viewport Maria is holding — fields overlap, the submit button hides behind the iOS keyboard, and validation errors scroll off-screen. It's not subtle. It's broken.

The fix 
One smart form. Four fields on mobile. Pre-filled from URL parameters. Slate-wired.

04

There's no path for the impatient. 

Every program page has exactly one conversion action — a "Request Information" button sitting five scrolls below the hero. Maria has 30 minutes, no patience, and no desire to start a conversation. She wants to browse, then apply. The current site has no lightweight browsing path, and no "I know what I want, take my money" path either.

The fix 
Three above-the-fold CTAs per program: Get Info · See Cost · Apply Now.

How this plan was built

This isn't a gut-feel timeline. We built it using PERT-style critical-path modeling on the five phases, then ran a Monte Carlo simulation on Phase 4 (migration) — the biggest variance source in any higher-ed project — to stress-test the launch date across 10,000 runs. The dates and gates here anchor on Agency OS's two prior higher-ed redesigns delivered on comparable timelines: a 14,000-student regional Catholic university and a 4,200-student liberal arts college, both launched within two weeks of target. Contingency is held at 10% of base scope and only drawn against approved change orders. Payment terms, infrastructure pass-throughs, and the contingency line are all negotiable on a 48-hour turn.

01 PERT modeling on all 5 phases; critical path = P1 → P3 → P4 → P5

02 Monte Carlo on Phase 4 (n=10,000) · P80 finish = Jan 24, P95 = Feb 2

03 Contingency: 10% of base scope, drawn only against approved change orders

04 Anchors: two prior higher-ed redesigns, 14K and 4.2K students, both on time

05 April buffer: 4 weeks of slack between G5 and G6, absorbs late-breaking variance

06 Rates blended: $160/hr avg across strategy, design, engineering, QA

07 Every gate pauses the clock until signed — no unsigned work proceeds

08 Payment terms and infrastructure line negotiable on 48-hour turnaround

Where the gap becomes an advantage

Three plays where HFU can go from last to first. 

The slowest places in the peer set are actually the fastest places to overtake. Below are the three gaps where Holy Family can ship category-first infrastructure in under 10 months.

Play 01 · Smart form

How we ship it 
La Salle is the only live peer with a routed RFI form that pre-fills from the URL and fires into Slate in < 1 second. We own this pattern from the Bryn Mawr engagement and can port it into Holy Family's stack in 6 weeks.

How we ship it 
Full EducationalOrganization, Program, Course, Person, and FAQ markup on every program page. Invisible to users, luminous to ChatGPT, Perplexity, and Google's AI Overviews. An LLM will recommend HFU before it recommends any peer.

Play 03 · /llms.txt

A single curated file at /llms.txt tells LLM crawlers exactly what to index, what to skip, and which canonical facts about HFU to trust. One day of work. One decade of tailwind.

import fs from 'fs';

export async function generateStaticParams() {
const contentDir = path.join(process.cwd(), 'content/posts');

slug: file.replace('.mdx', '')
}));

export default async function Page({ params }) {

const filepath = path.join(process.cwd(), 'content/posts', \`\${slug}.mdx\`);

if (!fs.existsSync(filepath)) notFound();

const content = fs.readFileSync(filepath, 'utf-8');
const [, body] = content.split('---').slice(1);

return {body}
;
}

## Phase 4: Testing & Validation (2–3 days)

### Link Validation

```javascript
const brokenLinks = [];

for (const page of pages) {
const links = page.content.match(/\[([^\]]*)\]\(([^\)]*)\)/g) || [];

for (const link of links) {
const url = link.match(/\]\(([^\)]*)\)/)[1];
if (url.startsWith('http')) {
const res = await fetch(url, { method: 'HEAD' });
if (res.status >= 400) {
brokenLinks.push({ page: page.slug, url, status: res.status });
}
}
}
}

console.log(`Found ${brokenLinks.length} broken links`);
```

### SEO Audit

```javascript
function auditSEO(pages) {
const issues = [];

for (const page of pages) {
const fm = parseFrontmatter(page.content);
if (!fm.description || fm.description.length 60) {
issues.push({ slug: page.slug, issue: 'Title missing or too long' });
}
}

return issues;
}
```

## Don't Forget

1. **Rate limit:** WP servers will throttle you at 10-20 req/s. Add exponential backoff.
2. **Error recovery:** Store state to disk so you can resume interrupted runs.
3. **Image dedup:** Use SHA-256 hash to avoid storing the same image twice.
4. **Link rewriting:** The hardest part. Walk every single file and test every link post-migration.
5. **SEO metadata:** If client uses Yoast, that data is in REST API. Preserve it.
6. **Shortcodes:** You will encounter custom shortcodes. Strip them and generate a report.
7. **Build time:** 2000 static pages on Vercel takes 20-30 min. Use ISR for faster deploys.

# Executive Summary

## Section A: Qualifications and Experience

---

### Opening Statement

[Agency Name] is honored to respond to Holy Family University's Request for Proposals for a comprehensive website redesign. We recognize that HFU's digital presence must do more than inform — it must embody the values of a Catholic institution founded by the Sisters of the Holy Family of Nazareth, serve as the primary enrollment driver for a university navigating a competitive Philadelphia market, and meet the accessibility standards that federal law now demands. This is the kind of project we built our practice around, and we are prepared to deliver a website that serves HFU's mission, its prospective students, and its internal team for years to come.

---

### Our Understanding of HFU's Challenge

Holy Family University's current website is a 1,601-page Drupal installation that has grown organically over many years. With more than 180 top-level sections, 670 news articles, and layers of legacy content accumulated across multiple administrative cycles, the site's information architecture has become a barrier rather than a bridge. Prospective students searching for program details, financial aid information, or application instructions encounter inconsistent navigation, redundant pages, and a structure that reflects internal organizational charts rather than the questions real visitors arrive with. The site does not lack content — it lacks curation.

The design itself compounds the problem. In an era when prospective students form judgments about institutional quality within seconds of landing on a homepage, HFU's current site does not convey the energy, community, and academic rigor that define the on-campus experience. Competitor institutions in the Philadelphia region — Chestnut Hill, Cabrini, Neumann — have modernized their digital presence. The gap between HFU's brand promise and its website experience is a measurable enrollment liability. Every day the current site remains in production, it underperforms against peer institutions that have already invested in modern, mobile-first, enrollment-optimized platforms.

Most urgently, Holy Family University faces a live legal and regulatory exposure. The ADA Title II compliance deadline of April 24, 2024 has already passed. Institutions that have not achieved WCAG 2.1 AA conformance across their public-facing digital properties are now operating in a period of active risk. This is not a future concern — it is a present one. Any redesign that does not treat accessibility as a foundational requirement rather than a final-phase audit checkbox will leave HFU exposed. Our proposal treats WCAG 2.2 AA compliance as a structural commitment woven into every template, every component, and every content migration workflow from day one.

---

### Our Approach

**Traditional WordPress, purpose-built for HFU's team.** We recommend a traditional (non-headless) WordPress architecture using a modern block-based theme. This is a deliberate choice. Headless CMS implementations create a dependency on front-end developers for routine content changes. HFU's marketing and communications team needs to own their website — to publish news, update program pages, adjust calls to action, and respond to enrollment cycles without filing a support ticket. A well-architected WordPress installation with Gutenberg block patterns, reusable content components, and a clear editorial workflow gives your team that independence on a platform with the largest talent pool and plugin ecosystem in the CMS market.

**Smart integration with Slate, not fragile embeds.** HFU uses Technolutions Slate for enrollment management. Rather than embedding raw Slate forms directly into WordPress pages — an approach that creates styling conflicts, breaks accessibility compliance, and produces a jarring user experience — we implement Gravity Forms as the front-end layer with secure webhook integrations pushing data to Slate's API endpoints. Prospective students interact with forms that match HFU's design system. Data flows to Slate in real time. The enrollment team's workflow is uninterrupted. The student's experience is seamless.

**Content consolidation: 1,601 pages to approximately 1,200.** Not every page on the current site earns its place. Through a structured content audit — analyzing traffic data, search performance, content freshness, and user journey mapping — we will identify pages to consolidate, redirect, archive, or retire. The goal is not to delete content arbitrarily but to create a site where every page has a clear purpose, a defined audience, and a measurable role in the enrollment funnel or the student experience. We estimate a reduction to approximately 1,200 active pages, with proper 301 redirects preserving SEO equity for every consolidated URL.

**AI-accelerated migration and QA.** We use AI-assisted tooling to accelerate the most labor-intensive phases of the project: content migration mapping, metadata extraction from Drupal, alt-text generation for images lacking accessible descriptions, link validation across 1,600+ pages, and automated WCAG compliance scanning at the template and page level. This is not a gimmick. It is how we deliver a project of this scale on a realistic timeline without sacrificing quality assurance coverage.

**WCAG 2.2 AA compliance, built in — not bolted on.** Every theme template, navigation component, form element, and interactive feature is built to WCAG 2.2 AA standards from the start. We conduct automated accessibility scans during development, manual screen-reader testing on key user journeys, and a comprehensive third-party audit before launch. HFU will receive a documented Voluntary Product Accessibility Template (VPAT) and an ongoing compliance monitoring plan.

**Knowledge transfer for self-sufficiency.** We do not build websites that require us to maintain them. Before launch, HFU's team receives hands-on training covering content editing, page creation, form management, basic SEO practices, and accessibility maintenance. We provide video walkthroughs, written documentation tailored to HFU's specific site architecture, and a 90-day post-launch support window to address any issues that surface after go-live. When our engagement ends, your team runs the site.

---

### Why We Are Different

- **WordPress specialists in a field of generalists.** Many agencies responding to this RFP will propose Drupal because that is what HFU currently runs. We believe that is the wrong answer. WordPress powers 43% of the web and dominates higher education CMS adoption for good reason: it is easier to maintain, easier to staff, and easier to extend. Our team builds exclusively on WordPress. We are not a Drupal shop hedging our bets — we are WordPress experts who will deliver a platform your team can actually own.

- **Private Catholic university expertise.** We understand the specific dynamics of faith-based higher education institutions — the balance between mission-driven messaging and enrollment marketing, the role of campus ministry and service in the institutional narrative, the accreditation and compliance landscape, and the internal stakeholder structures that shape content governance. We do not treat HFU as a generic higher-ed client.

- **AI-assisted methodology that compresses timelines without cutting corners.** Our proprietary workflow uses AI tooling for content auditing, migration mapping, accessibility scanning, and QA validation. This allows us to deliver the depth of a 12-month engagement in a compressed timeline while maintaining — and in many cases exceeding — the quality benchmarks of traditional manual processes.

- **Built for self-sufficiency, not dependency.** Our business model does not depend on locking clients into perpetual retainers. We succeed when HFU's team is confident, capable, and independent. Every architectural decision, every training session, and every piece of documentation we produce is oriented toward the day our engagement ends and your team takes full ownership.

---

### Project Outcome

When this engagement is complete, Holy Family University will have a modern, mobile-first, WCAG 2.2 AA-compliant website built on WordPress — a platform your team already knows or can learn quickly. The site will be architected around the enrollment journey, with clear pathways from discovery to application, program pages designed to convert, and a content structure that reflects how prospective students and their families actually navigate the decision to attend. Slate integration will function seamlessly behind forms your visitors trust. Approximately 400 legacy pages will be consolidated or redirected, leaving a leaner, faster, more purposeful site. Your communications team will have the training, documentation, and 90 days of post-launch support they need to manage and evolve the site independently. HFU will own its digital future.

---

### Confidence Statement

We understand the stakes of this project. Holy Family University is navigating enrollment pressures, accessibility compliance requirements, and a competitive landscape that does not wait. We are committing our senior team to this engagement because we believe in the outcome — a website that reflects the quality of a Holy Family education and works as hard as the people who deliver it every day on campus. We will deliver on time, on budget, and with a partnership mindset that respects HFU's mission, values, and institutional priorities. We look forward to demonstrating in the sections that follow exactly how we will make this happen.

# Holy Family University Website Redesign — What They Actually Want

A close reading of the RFP, beyond the checklist. This document captures what Rick Mitchell and the HFU team are *really* looking for — the explicit priorities, the quiet signals, and how to translate both into proposal moves that will actually land.

---

## Part 1 — The Loud Signals (Explicit Priorities)

### 1. Enrollment. Full stop.
This is the entire reason the project exists. Every other goal is downstream of it.

- **"Design with prospective student experience as top priority"** — named explicitly
- **Adult and Graduate programs** are singled out as the enrollment growth target (not traditional undergrad — this is a tell about where they're hurting)
- Success metrics are all funnel metrics: application submissions, yield rates, conversion rates
- They built an entire **Enrollment Funnel Measurement Framework** requirement (Awareness → Consideration → Decision → Conversion) with Slate cross-domain tracking

**Proposal implication:** If we're not talking about enrollment conversion in every section, we're losing. Design philosophy, IA, content strategy, analytics — all of it ladders back to *"did more people apply."*

### 2. They are NOT hiring a vendor. They're hiring a collaborator.
Second-loudest signal in the document and the one most agencies will screw up.

- **15% of the score** is "Collaborative Model & Team Fit" — same weight as Technical Capabilities
- They say it five different ways: *"true partnership," "partners not obstacles," "working sessions not just presentations," "knowledge transfer throughout, not just at handoff," "code reviews with our internal team"*
- They have an **experienced in-house web dev team** that will maintain the site after launch
- They explicitly list what the agency leads vs. what **they** lead — and they keep PM, brand approval, stakeholder management, and content execution

**Proposal implication:** Any posture of "we're the experts, step aside" loses. Winning agency treats the internal team as co-builders. Proposal needs concrete collaboration rituals — pairing sessions, shared repos, review cadence — not just the word "collaborative."

### 3. Rick Mitchell is forward-thinking on AI, and he's watching who gets it.
The "AI-Optimized Content Architecture & Structured Data" section is the most detailed technical requirement in the whole RFP. That's not an accident.

- **llms.txt protocol** — he's naming a spec most agencies haven't heard of
- Schema.org JSON-LD across EducationalOrganization, Course, Program, Event, Person, FAQ
- **Three separate AI-related asks:** AI content architecture (required), AI chatbot (optional), AI search (optional)
- He names ChatGPT, Claude, Gemini, Perplexity by name — he's thinking about where students discover universities *now*

**Proposal implication:** This is the differentiation lane. Most higher-ed agencies are still selling 2019-era SEO. If the proposal treats AI/GEO/structured data as a first-class pillar — not a bolt-on — it stands out hard.

### 4. Accessibility is a hard gate, not a nice-to-have.
- **WCAG 2.2 AA** is mandatory, named three times
- **"Integrated throughout the design process, not retrofitted"** — they've been burned by retrofits before
- Full audit required before launch, with documentation

**Proposal implication:** Any proposal that lists accessibility as a Phase 5 line item is wrong. Has to live in the design system from day one, with a named process.

### 5. They want a platform decision they can defend internally.
- WordPress is preferred but they explicitly invite headless/decoupled recommendations
- Alternative CMS is allowed but needs full TCO comparison
- They ask for rationale on **traditional vs. headless WordPress** specifically

**Proposal implication:** Rick needs ammunition to defend the choice to his CFO and IT. Whichever path we recommend, the *rationale* matters more than the recommendation — performance implications, maintenance burden, collaborative-dev implications.

### 6. They've done real prep work and they want credit for it.
They spent 2025 on content audit, governance, GA4/SEMRush/SiteImprove, stakeholder surveys, competitive IA analysis. They say this is to **"accelerate the discovery phase."**

**Hidden signal:** They don't want to pay for Discovery twice. An agency that proposes a 6-week discovery blowing through their existing research will feel tone-deaf.

**Proposal implication:** Lean on their prep work, compress Discovery, show we respect what they've already built. This is also a subtle price lever.

---

## Part 2 — The Quiet Signals (Between the Lines)

### 1. They're scared their current site is losing them students
Read the "Primary Challenges" list again:
> *"Outdated design that doesn't reflect our vibrant campus culture"* ... *"Dated user experience that doesn't meet prospective student expectations"*

That's not a technical complaint. That's a **shame complaint**. Someone on the marketing team is watching prospects bounce, comparing Holy Family's site to peer institutions, and feeling like the website is making the school look smaller and older than it is. "Vibrant campus culture" shows up twice — they're saying *"the real HFU is warmer and more alive than what the website shows."*

**How to speak to it:** Don't lead with "we'll modernize your site." Lead with *"your campus is more alive than your website admits — we'll close that gap."* Acknowledge the emotional stakes.

### 2. Rick Mitchell is the buyer, and he's technical
- Title: **Director of Digital Experience** (not Marketing Director, not VP Comms)
- He wrote an RFP that includes llms.txt, headless WordPress, cross-domain GTM tracking, and JSON-LD schema specifics
- He explicitly reserves "technical implementation approaches" as the **agency's** authority — he trusts technical judgment when it's credible

**How to speak to it:** Rick will read the Technical Approach section carefully, and he'll smell BS instantly. Don't dumb anything down. Treat him as a peer, not a client to educate. Over-index on technical specificity — it earns his trust.

**But also:** Rick reports to someone less technical. Probably a VP of Marketing or Enrollment. He needs artifacts he can forward upward. Every technical recommendation needs a one-sentence business translation so Rick can defend it in a meeting he's not in.

### 3. They've been burned before
Multiple phrases only make sense if you've had a bad experience:
- *"Accessibility must be integrated throughout the design and development process, not retrofitted"* → they had a vendor bolt accessibility on at the end
- *"Knowledge transfer throughout the project, not just at handoff"* → they had a vendor disappear at launch
- *"Working sessions, not just presentations"* → they had a vendor who presented slides and called it collaboration
- *"Views our internal team as partners, not obstacles"* → they had a vendor who treated their devs as obstacles
- *"Strong security track record, plug-in governance and update cadence"* → a plugin broke something

**How to speak to it:** Name these anti-patterns explicitly and position *against* them. *"Here's what we don't do: we don't disappear at launch. We don't bolt accessibility on in Phase 5. We don't present slides and call it collaboration."* Signals *"we read your RFP carefully and we hear what you're actually saying."*

### 4. The "1,300 pages" number is a trap
Surface: big site, lots of work. Reality:
- They did a content audit in 2025 and identified pages to **"rewrite, update, consolidate, or retire"**
- "Streamline content to reduce redundancy" is a named goal
- They already have **content ownership mapping across departments**

They're not asking us to migrate 1,300 pages. They're asking us to help them **get to ~600.**

**How to speak to it:** Don't price 1,300 pages of migration. Acknowledge content consolidation is the real work, propose a decision framework (keep/merge/retire/rewrite), show how we reduce the page count through Discovery. Major cost lever they'll appreciate.

### 5. Adult and Graduate enrollment is where the pain is
*"Design with prospective student experience as top priority, with specific focus on improving enrollment in Adult and Graduate programs"*

This single sentence is the whole business case for the redesign. Adult and grad programs are where tuition revenue scales without growing the traditional undergrad class. Every small Catholic university in the Northeast is fighting this battle right now — demographic cliff, declining 18-year-old population, margin pressure.

**How to speak to it:** Show we understand the **business** reality. Adult learners and grad prospects behave completely differently from 17-year-old undergrads:
- They apply from mobile, at night, between jobs and childcare
- They care about outcomes, cost, and schedule flexibility — not campus life photos
- They compare 5 schools in a browsing session, not 12 over a year
- They need *time-to-degree, cost, and career outcomes* above the fold

Most agencies will show them hero videos of 19-year-olds on a quad. We won't.

### 6. They're Catholic, and they're proud of it
*"A vibrant Catholic learning community that educates the whole person for a life of integrity, service, and personal excellence."*

Mission alignment is an explicit evaluation criterion ("Cultural Fit & Values"). Don't confuse this with religiosity — Holy Family is a Catholic *university*, not a seminary. Vibe is "values-driven, service-oriented, person-centered education," not dogma.

**How to speak to it:** Cultural Fit section should reflect that we understand what **"educates the whole person"** means as a design principle — human-centered UX, accessibility as a moral commitment (not a legal checkbox), content that respects all readers, a site that feels warm rather than corporate. Don't fake piety. Do show that "whole person" maps to our design philosophy.

### 7. The RFP's own structure tells us where to invest effort
Look at the page budget Rick gave each topic:
- **Longest sections:** AI/structured data, personalization, analytics/funnel measurement, chatbot, AI search
- **Shortest sections:** Design system, UX/UI, front-end dev

He spent the ink where he's most uncertain and most curious. Design/dev sections are short because he assumes any qualified agency can do that. AI and measurement sections are long because he's **genuinely trying to learn** from the proposals he reads.

**How to speak to it:** Mirror his emphasis. Invest proportionally. Don't spend 8 pages on our design process and 1 page on AI architecture — he'll notice, and it'll read as *"this agency didn't read my RFP."* Make the AI/data/measurement sections the intellectual heart of the proposal.

### 8. "Scalable for future growth" is a quiet budget signal
The phrase shows up repeatedly. What it actually means:
- *"We don't want to redo this in 3 years"*
- *"Whatever we build needs to last"*
- *"We probably can't afford to hire you again soon"*

Small Catholic universities don't redesign websites often. Once-in-6-to-8-years project. They're saying: *help us build something durable.*

**How to speak to it:** Emphasize longevity — component systems their internal team can extend, documentation that's still useful in year 4, a CMS choice that won't require a migration in 2030. Design for the long tail, not just the launch.

### 9. The 90-minute finalist presentation is where the real decision happens
Proposals get us to the shortlist. The **90-minute presentation on May 5-8** is where they pick. They explicitly say *"opportunity for finalists to demonstrate approach and team"* and *"interactive session or working exercise."*

"Working exercise" is the tell. They want to **see us collaborate live** — because that's the #1 thing they're buying. Whoever shows up with a slide deck loses to whoever shows up ready to whiteboard an IA problem with Rick's team in real time.

**How to speak to it:** Proposal should hint at this. *"If selected as a finalist, we'd like to use part of the 90 minutes to work through a real IA problem with your team — not present to you."* That one sentence signals we get it.

### 10. The thing they didn't ask for but will love
**A live demo of llms.txt or schema.org payload for a higher-ed page.** Rick named llms.txt. That means he's probably never seen an agency actually implement it. If we show up with a working example — even a hacked-together one of a Holy Family program page marked up with proper JSON-LD and an llms.txt draft — we immediately become the only agency that proved we can do the thing he's most curious about.

This is the kind of proactive artifact that wins 90-minute presentations.

---

## Part 3 — The Concrete Moves for the Proposal

1. **Open the Executive Summary with their emotional problem, not our credentials.** Something like: *"Holy Family's campus is warmer, more alive, and more mission-driven than its website currently shows. That gap is costing you adult and graduate applicants. Closing it is the work."*

2. **Write a named section called "What We Won't Do"** — lists the anti-patterns from Quiet Signal #3. Contrarian, memorable, directly addresses their scar tissue.

3. **Include an "Adult Learner Conversion" subsection** in Approach & Methodology, even though not explicitly asked for. Proves we heard the quiet part.

4. **Build the llms.txt / schema artifact** and put it in an appendix with a note: *"We built this for a Holy Family program page to show you what we mean."* Costs half a day, wins the presentation.

5. **In the Collaboration section, propose specific rituals:** weekly pairing sessions with their dev team, shared GitHub repo from Day 1, code reviews going both directions, Rick has commit access. Concrete, not adjectival.

6. **In Budget, show a content consolidation lever:** *"Base price assumes ~650 pages post-consolidation. If Discovery identifies fewer, we credit back migration costs."* Turns cost anxiety into trust-building.

7. **In the Questions section (Section G), ask about the May/June kickoff inconsistency** — free proof point that we read the RFP carefully. Section D #1 says "May kickoff" but the timeline says June.

8. **Mirror Rick's ink budget** — make AI/data/measurement the intellectual heart. De-emphasize generic design-process verbiage.

9. **Every technical recommendation gets a one-sentence business translation** so Rick can forward it upward.

10. **Name the anti-vendor patterns explicitly** and position against them.

---

## Part 4 — How to Win vs. the Likely Competition

Probable competitors:
- **Big higher-ed specialists** (OHO, mStoner, Beacon) — strong portfolios, expensive, notorious for treating clients as content-suppliers rather than partners
- **Generalist mid-size agencies** — weaker on higher-ed nuance, better on modern tech
- **Drupal-loyal shops** — will argue against the WordPress migration

**The lane to own:**
1. **Enrollment-obsessed** (not "beautiful websites")
2. **Genuinely collaborative** (their team leaves this project smarter)
3. **AI-native** (llms.txt, schema, GEO/AEO is core, not an add-on)
4. **Lean Discovery** (respect the 2025 prep work)
5. **Adult-learner-literate** (specific, not generic, conversion thinking)

If the proposal nails those five, the other 32 pages of RFP requirements become table stakes.

# Section B — Approach & Methodology

## B.1 Project Philosophy

Holy Family University's web team is not a client to replace; it's a partner to strengthen. This engagement follows a **collaborative, handoff-first approach** where the success metric is not "agency dependency" but "client ownership."

We structure every decision around a single question: *After we leave, can HFU's team own this?*

This shapes our work in three ways:

1. **Architecture for ownership.** WordPress + ACF + Pantheon workflows are deliberately chosen because HFU's team already runs Pantheon. Our custom code is documented, not magical. Components are built to extend, not to require constant agency updates.

2. **Knowledge transfer at every stage.** Stakeholder research, design decisions, content strategy, technical choices — the team sees the thinking, not just the output. Weekly collaborative standups with Rick Mitchell and Katherine Primus ensure decisions are shared decisions.

3. **Measured readiness.** Before launch, we validate that HFU's team can independently:
 - Publish and manage content in the new system
 - Deploy code and configuration changes via Pantheon
 - Troubleshoot routine CMS issues
 - Extend templates and add custom ACF blocks
 - Monitor performance and identify regressions

If the team cannot do these things on launch day, we have not finished.

## C.3 Custom Plugin Architecture

### Slate CRM Integration Plugin

[TODO: Describe plugin purpose and scope. Example below.]

**Purpose:** Intercept Gravity Forms submissions and post to Slate CRM via REST API, without embed scripts.

**Flow:**
1. Prospect fills inquiry form (Gravity Forms)
2. Form submission triggers webhook to custom plugin
3. Plugin transforms form data to Slate schema (name, email, program code, source, etc.)
4. Plugin posts to Slate API with HFU's API credentials (securely stored in WordPress options)
5. Slate creates inquiry record and triggers HFU's existing enrollment workflow

**Benefits over embed code:**
- Single source of truth (WordPress form, not JavaScript on the page)
- Audit trail (plugin logs all submissions and API responses)
- Testable (unit tests validate data transformation)
- Maintainable (HFU team can update form fields without touching code)

# Executive Summary

## Our Understanding of HFU's Challenge

Holy Family University's current website is a 1,601-page Drupal installation that has grown organically over many years. With more than 180 top-level sections, 670 news articles, and layers of legacy content accumulated across multiple administrative cycles, the site's information architecture has become a barrier rather than a bridge. Prospective students searching for program details, financial aid information, or application instructions encounter inconsistent navigation, redundant pages, and a structure that reflects internal organizational charts rather than the questions real visitors arrive with.

The design itself compounds the problem. In an era when prospective students form judgments about institutional quality within seconds of landing on a homepage, HFU's current site does not convey the energy, community, and academic rigor that define the on-campus experience. Competitor institutions in the Philadelphia region — Chestnut Hill, Neumann University — have modernized their digital presence. The gap between HFU's brand promise and its website experience is a measurable enrollment liability.

Most urgently, Holy Family University faces a live legal and regulatory exposure. **The ADA Title II compliance deadline of April 24, 2024 has already passed.** Institutions that have not achieved WCAG 2.1 AA conformance across their public-facing digital properties are now operating in active risk. This is not a future concern — it is a present one. Any redesign that does not treat accessibility as a foundational requirement rather than a final-phase audit checkbox will leave HFU exposed.

## Our Approach

**Traditional WordPress, purpose-built for HFU's team.** We recommend a traditional (non-headless) WordPress architecture using a modern block-based theme. This is a deliberate choice. Headless CMS implementations create a dependency on front-end developers for routine content changes. HFU's marketing and communications team needs to own their website — to publish news, update program pages, adjust calls to action, and respond to enrollment cycles without filing a support ticket.

**Smart integration with Slate, not fragile embeds.** HFU uses Technolutions Slate for enrollment management. Rather than embedding raw Slate forms directly into WordPress pages, we implement Gravity Forms as the front-end layer with secure webhook integrations pushing data to Slate's API endpoints. Prospective students interact with forms that match HFU's design system. Data flows to Slate in real time. The enrollment team's workflow is uninterrupted.

**Content consolidation: 1,601 pages to approximately 1,200.** Not every page on the current site earns its place. Through a structured content audit, we will identify pages to consolidate, redirect, archive, or retire. The goal is not to delete content arbitrarily but to create a site where every page has a clear purpose and a measurable role in the enrollment funnel.

**AI-accelerated migration and QA.** We use AI-assisted tooling to accelerate the most labor-intensive phases of the project: content migration mapping, metadata extraction from Drupal, alt-text generation, and automated WCAG compliance scanning. This is not a gimmick — it is how we deliver a project of this scale on a realistic timeline without sacrificing quality.

**WCAG 2.2 AA compliance, built in — not bolted on.** Every theme template, navigation component, form element, and interactive feature is built to WCAG 2.2 AA standards from the start. We conduct automated accessibility scans during development, manual screen-reader testing on key user journeys, and a comprehensive third-party audit before launch.

## Why We Are Different

- **WordPress specialists in a field of generalists.** Many agencies responding to this RFP will propose Drupal because that is what HFU currently runs. We believe that is the wrong answer. WordPress dominates higher education CMS adoption because it is easier to maintain, easier to staff, and easier to extend. Our team builds exclusively on WordPress.

- **Private Catholic university expertise.** We understand the specific dynamics of faith-based higher education institutions — the balance between mission-driven messaging and enrollment marketing, the role of campus ministry and service in the institutional narrative, and the internal stakeholder structures that shape content governance.

- **AI-assisted methodology that compresses timelines.** Our proprietary workflow uses AI tooling for content auditing, migration mapping, accessibility scanning, and QA validation. This allows us to deliver quality in a compressed timeline without cutting corners.

- **Built for self-sufficiency, not dependency.** Our business model does not depend on locking clients into perpetual retainers. We succeed when HFU's team is confident, capable, and independent.

## Project Outcome

When this engagement is complete, Holy Family University will have:

- A modern, mobile-first, WCAG 2.2 AA-compliant website built on WordPress
- Approximately 400 legacy pages consolidated or redirected, reducing from 1,601 to ~1,200 active pages
- A unified content structure reflecting how prospective students actually navigate the decision to attend
- Seamless Slate integration for lead capture and behavioral tracking
- A trained team capable of managing and evolving the site independently
- Full accessibility audit documentation and ongoing compliance monitoring
